coredata.dc:title: Controlled synthesis of fluorescent carbon materials with the assistance of capillary electrophoresis
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Lizhi
Zhao, Lei
Li, Hongli
Ma, Jie
Bian, Lei
Wang, Xiayan
Pu, Qiaosheng
coredata.dc:description: Carbon nanodots (CNDs) have been widely applied in variety of fields, while some evidences indicate their components may be complicated. In this work, capillary electrophoresis (CE) was used to evaluate the effect of synthetic conditions of fluorescent CNDs prepared through the hydrothermal method using citric acid (CA) and Triaminoguanidinium chloride (TGCl) as the starting materials. The results indicated that the fluorescent components of the products were affected by the ratio of the starting materials, the reaction temperature and reaction time. Under selected conditions, a ratio of TGCl to CA of 1:6, the reaction at 180 °C for 3 h, the product contains more than 4 fluorescent components with similar optical properties. CNDs were used for the determination of Cr(VI) in environmental samples with recoveries ranging in 95.3–107%, and the mechanism was also confirmed.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanodots
Electrophoresis
Inner filter effect
Environmental pollution
Fluorescence sensing
Cr(VI) sensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effect of reaction condition reflected by the fluorescence spectrometry
Effect of the synthesis condition reflected by CE

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China [Nos. 21575054 , 21625501 , and 21527808 ], and the Beijing Outstanding Young Scientists Program [No. BJJWZYJH01201910005017 ].

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence intensity (excited at 340 nm) under different (a) molar ratio of TGCl to CA, (b) reaction temperature, and (c) reaction time. Error bar: (±) SD, n = 3.

Electropherograms of CNDs synthesized with different molar ratio of TGCl to CA (the molarity of TGCl maintained constant and that of CA increased). The running buffer is 10 mM borax. The capillary total length is 68 cm and effective separation length is 18 cm, the inner diameter is 75 μm, the excitation and emission wavelengths of fluorescence detector were set at 340 nm and 410 nm. Enlarged versions of typical electropherograms are shown at the right panel.

Electropherograms of CNDs (molar ratio of TGCl to CA is 1:6) synthesized under different (a) reaction temperature and (b) reaction time. (c) Electropherograms of CNDs synthesized with excessive TGCl. Electrophoretic conditions were the same as in Fig. 2.

Electropherograms of CNDs (a) in the first case with a fixed emission wavelength of 410 nm and (b) in the second case with a fixed excitation wavelength of 340 nm. Electrophoretic conditions were the same as in Fig. 2.

(a) The UV–Vis spectrum, fluorescence spectra and (b) the normalized spectra of the CNDs. (c) The TEM image of CNDs, inset figure is the diameter distribution by DLS. There are about 60 particles in this image, 4 of them were highlighted by red circles to facilitate the identification of these particles. (d) The FTIR spectrum of CNDs. (e) The XPS full spectrum, high resolution XPS spectra of (f) C 1s, (g) N 1s and (h) O 1s. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Effects of (a) various pH, (b) temperature and (c) illumination time on fluorescence intensity of CNDs. Error bar: (±) SD, n = 3.

Selectivity of Cr(VI) towards (a) anion interferences (number 1–17 represent blank, Cr(VI), H2PO4 −, HCO3 −, P2O7 4−, NO3 −, CO3 2−, CH3COO−, Cl−, F−, SO4 2−, HPO4 2−, HCOO−, ClO3 −, Br−, ClO4 −, and PO4 3−, respectively.) and (b) cation interferences (number 1–18 represent blank, Cr(VI), Li+, Na+, K+, Ag+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Ca2+, Mn2+, Cu2+, Ba2+, Mg2+, Pb2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Al3+, and Fe3+, respectively.) (c) The fluorescence spectra of CNDs upon addition of different concentrations of Cr(VI). (d) The relationship between (F0–F)/F0 and Cr(VI) concentration in the range of 0.2–500 μM, inset figure is the linear relationship. Error bar: (±) SD, n = 3.

(a) The quenching efficiency (E%) of observed and corrected fluorescence intensity after addition of different concentrations of Cr(VI). (b) The Stern-Volmer plots at four temperatures. Error bar: (±) SD, n = 3.
Synthesis of blue-emitting fluorescent CNDs by hydrothermal treatment using TGCl and CA.
Detection of Cr(VI) in water and soil samples.
Comparison of different methods for Cr(VI) sensing.

row.entry: High sensitivity, expensive instrument, need preconcentration
PANI/GQDs (for the modification of carbon electrode)
Low sensitivity, need modification of the electrode, short analysis time (<1 min)
Long response time (20 min), narrow linear range
Free of heavy metal, low sensitivity, long synthesis time of SQDs (>72 h)
Rapid response (5 min), long synthesis time of MOF (72 h)
Mild and rapid synthesis (55 °C, 30 min), rapid response, low sensitivity
Rapid response (1 min), acceptable sensitivity, simple operation

head.ce:title: Controlled synthesis of fluorescent carbon materials with the assistance of capillary electrophoresis

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry, Key Laboratory of Nonferrous Metals Chemistry and Resources Utilization of Gansu Province, Department of Chemistry, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou, Gansu, 730000, China

Key Laboratory of Nonferrous Metals Chemistry and Resources Utilization of Gansu Province

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemical Engineering and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center of Excellence for Environmental Safety and Biological Effects, Beijing Key Laboratory for Green Catalysis and Separation, Department of Chemistry and Biology, Beijing University of Technology, Beijing, 100124, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Center of Excellence for Environmental Safety and Biological Effects
Beijing Key Laboratory for Green Catalysis and Separation

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Center of Excellence for Environmental Safety and Biological Effects, Beijing Key Laboratory for Green Catalysis and Separation, Department of Chemistry and Biology, Beijing University of Technology, Beijing 100124, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon nanodots (CNDs) have been widely applied in variety of fields, while some evidences indicate their components may be complicated. In this work, capillary electrophoresis (CE) was used to evaluate the effect of synthetic conditions of fluorescent CNDs prepared through the hydrothermal method using citric acid (CA) and Triaminoguanidinium chloride (TGCl) as the starting materials. The results indicated that the fluorescent components of the products were affected by the ratio of the starting materials, the reaction temperature and reaction time. Under selected conditions, a ratio of TGCl to CA of 1:6, the reaction at 180 °C for 3 h, the product contains more than 4 fluorescent components with similar optical properties. CNDs were used for the determination of Cr(VI) in environmental samples with recoveries ranging in 95.3–107%, and the mechanism was also confirmed.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent materials have been a focus of a prodigious number of researches. Considerable fluorescent materials such as metal nanoclusters (NCs) [1], nanodiamonds and silicon quantum dots [2], carbon nanodots (CNDs) [3], polymer nanoparticles [4,5], fluorescent covalent organic frameworks (COFs) [6], and metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) [7], and inorganic semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) [8] have been synthesized for various applications. Among them, CNDs have attracted intensive attention and unique properties such as excellent optical property, good water solubility, biocompatibility, chemical inertness, easy surface functionalization, high resistance to photobleaching, low cost of starting materials, good stability and eco-friendly synthesis [9] have been reported.

Cost-efficient, simple large scale synthesis of CNDs could be carried out by either bottom-up or top-down approaches [10]. However, CNDs obtained from above approaches tend to display a rather complex and heterogeneous characteristic in terms of size distribution and surface functionality [11]. The formation of molecular fluorophores, which are potentially oligomeric or polymeric, was ascertained for CNDs prepared with a bottom-up synthesis by Baker et al. [12]. Urban's group [13] constructed a model system to simulate optical properties of CNDs and expounded that excitation dependence of the emission could come from a selective excitation of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) with slightly different energy gaps and from energy transfer among them. Righetto et al. [14] revealed that molecular byproducts that were freely moving in the solution rather than that attached to the carbonaceous scaffold played a dominated role for the emission properties of CNDs. Fortunately, although CNDs are more probably complex obtained by a one-pot synthesis [9], attractive applications in variety of fields including chemical sensing [15], bioimaging [16], drug delivery [17], photocatalysis [18], LED devices [19], and many others have been reported. To prepare CNDs for a specific property or application, an analysis tool that can precisely recognize individual species is needed.

Fluorescence spectroscopy is the widely applied technique for the characterization of fluorescent CNDs, but considering the complexity of the components of CNDs, it merely provides the summation or average properties of all components [20], which may give a biased result if the needed property is from a specific component. Isolation of the specific component is surely the best way, but due to the fact that photoluminescence mechanism of CNDs is not clear [9,10], separation to obtain the pure fluorescent component is a challenge. Until now, techniques including hydrophilicity gradient ultracentrifugation separation [21], polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (PAGE) [22], anion exchange high-performance liquid chromatography (AE-HPLC) [23,24], HPLC-MS coupling technique [25,26], low-pressure gel permeation chromatography [27], silica gel column chromatography [28], and capillary electrophoresis (CE) [11,20,29,30] have been adopted to separate and characterize CNDs. Considering the complexity of CNDs and operation convenience of the analysis, CE may be the most suitable technique for this purpose because 1) small molecules, macromolecules and even particles can be analyzed quickly and efficiently using CE in a single run; 2) separation can be performed in pure aqueous solution, which is highly compatible with CNDs; 3) an open-tubular channel is normally used, which greatly reduces the possibility of contamination or blockage of the column by particles.

In this work, with the aid of CE, we synthesized a series of brilliantly blue-emitting nitrogen-doped fluorescent CNDs by convenient bottom-up hydrothermal treatment using citric acid (CA) and Triaminoguanidinium chloride (TGCl). Fluorescence spectrometry could reflect the variation of total fluorescence of the obtained products under different conditions but it could not illustrate the change in the components. Through CE with a fluorescence detector, the effects of reaction conditions on the composition of the products were clearly determined. CE with a UV detector also revealed the presence of non-fluorescent components. Based on these results, CNDs suitable for the determination of Cr(VI), which is toxic to humans, were prepared and successfully used as an on-off chemosensor of Cr(VI). A good linear relationship between quenching efficiency and concentration of Cr(VI) was obtained in a range of 0.2–80 μM with a limit of detection of 0.11 μM, which provides a good feasibility for accurate detection of Cr(VI) in environmental samples.

Guanidine hydrochloride was purchased from Sinopharm chemical reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). 1,4-dioxane was purchased from Damao chemical reagent factory (Tianjin, China). Citric acid (CA) was obtained from Shuangshuang chemical reagent Co., Ltd. (Yantai, China). Hydrazine hydrate (N2H4) and concentrated hydrochloric acid (HCl) were bought from Lian long bohua pharmaceutical chemical Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Tris-(hydroxymethyl) aminomethane (Tris) and XAD-7 resin were obtained from Sigma Aldrich Co. (Shanghai, China). Solutions of Li+, Na+, K+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Ca2+, Mn2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, Al3+, Cu2+ and Co2+ were prepared from their chloride salts. Ag+, Ba2+, Pb2+ and Cr3+ aqueous solutions were prepared from their nitrate salts. Cr(VI) solutions were prepared using its potassium salt, all other solutions of anions were prepared from their sodium salts. All the chemicals were of analytical grade or higher and used without further purification. Deionized (DI) water purified by the Millipore purification system was used in all experimental procedures.

A FEI Tecnai G2 F30 transmission electron microscope (TEM) operated at 200 kV was used to observe the sizes and morphologies of the materials. The size distribution of materials was measured on a BI-200SM dynamic laser light scattering (DLS) instrument (Brookhaven, USA). The fluorescence and the UV–Vis absorption spectra were measured on a F97Pro fluorescence spectrophotometer (Lengguang Technology Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China) and a UV 2800SPC spectrophotometer (Hengping Scientific Instrument Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China), respectively. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was acquired on PHI-5720 multifunction spectrum instrument (Spectra-Physics Inc., USA). Fourier transform infrared spectrometry (FTIR) was recorded on a Nexus 670 spectrometer (Nicolet, USA). Fluorescence lifetime measurements was conducted by a time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) system coupled with FLS920 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Edinburgh Instruments, West Lothian, U.K).

CE was performed with a high-voltage module (DW-N303-1C42, Dongwen High-Voltage Power Supply, Tianjin, China) and a fluorescence detector (Shimadzu RF-530) or a UV absorbance detector (LINEAR UVIS 200CE). To accommodate the capillary, the original flow cell was removed and the capillary was fitted with a pair of capillary sleeves. The electropherograms were fed to a NI USB-6009 data acquisition card (National Instruments, Austin, TX, USA) displayed and stored in a computer by a LabVIEW program that also controlled the HV output.

TGCl was prepared employing N2H4 and guanidine hydrochloride in the solvent of 1,4-dioxane on the condition of refluxing as reported previously [31]. Briefly, 1.91 g of guanidine hydrochloride was added into 10 mL of 1,4-dioxane under normal stirring condition. Then 3.41 g of N2H4 was added into the mixture. After refluxing for 2 h, the mixture was cooled to room temperature naturally, and the white solid was filtered and washed with 1,4-dioxane several times to remove excess N2H4 and dried.

The fluorescent CNDs were prepared through a simple hydrothermal method. Typically, 0.14 g of TGCl powder and 1.26 g of CA were placed in 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave, then 20 mL ultrapure water containing 10 mg/mL NaOH was added. The raw materials were dissolved sufficiently under ultrasonication. The autoclave was heated at 180 °C for 3 h. After it cooled to room temperature, a yellowish-brown solution was obtained. The solution was centrifuged and filtered with 0.22 μm membranes twice for subsequent use.

For CE with a fluorescence detector, a bare silica capillary with total length of 68 cm (inner diameter is 75 μm, outer diameter is 365 μm) was pretreated with 1 M NaOH for 15 min. The capillary was then flushed with water and working buffer for 20 min, respectively. Aqueous fluorescent CNDs samples in 10 mM borax buffer were injected electro dynamically at a voltage of 5 kV for 20 s and then separated at a voltage of 17 kV. Short-end injection was used for all samples and the effective separation length is 18 cm. The excitation and emission wavelengths of the fluorescence detector were 340 nm and 410 nm, respectively. Between runs, the capillary was flushed with borax buffer for 5 min to restore the surface of the capillary. 3 runs were made for each sample.

For CE with a UV detector, the length of the capillary was 44 cm (effective length of 18 cm). Aqueous CNDs samples were injected at a voltage of 3 kV for 20 s and separated at a voltage of 11 kV. The detection wavelength was 210 nm. Other conditions were the same as CE with fluorescence detection.

The as-prepared CNDs solution was diluted in Tris-HCl buffer solution (10 mM, pH 8.0) 1/50 to obtain the stock solution. To evaluate the fluorescence quenching by Cr(VI), 500 μL of the CNDs stock solution was added into 5 mL centrifuge tubes, then different concentrations of Cr(VI) were added into these tubes. Then the resulting solutions were mixed by vortex vibration and kept at room temperature for 5 min. The fluorescence emission spectra were recorded with an excitation at 340 nm. All measurements were repeated thrice.

Two kinds of water samples (tap water and Yellow River water) were selected for the analysis of Cr(VI). The water samples were centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 20 min to remove large insoluble particles and sediments and subsequently filtered through 0.22 μm membranes. The detection procedure for treated water samples was the same as the quenching assay mentioned above.

Two kinds of soil samples including a Campus vegetation soil and a Yellow River shoreside sediment were collected from a lawn in the main campus of Lanzhou University and the shore of the Yellow River in Lanzhou district, respectively. The soil samples were pretreated by a previously reported method with little modification [32]. The collected soil samples were dried at 60 °C and grinded with a mortar and pestle. For each sample, 60 mg soil and 405 mg MgCl2 were mixed and dissolved in 50 mL extractant of 0.28 M Na2CO3 and 0.5 M NaOH. Subsequently, the solution was boiled for 20 min. After cooling, it was filtered through filter paper and washed with DI water for three times, then the filtrate was combined and adjusted to 100 mL with DI water. Next, 2 g activated XAD-7 resin was added into the filtrate and adjusted pH to 3.0 with 6 M HCl. After shaking for 5 min, it was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane and pH was adjusted to 8.0 for analysis following the procedure mentioned above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of reaction condition reflected by the fluorescence spectrometry
ce:section.ce:para: Hydrothermal method has been widely used for synthesis of various fluorescent materials. The aim of this work is to prepare a novel material that can be used for the detection of CrO4 2−, so a nitrogen (N)-rich compound TGCl was selected to endow the product a cationic property, while CA was chosen as the carbon source because of its popularity as a starting material and its special properties such as nucleation [33]. N was widely used for the chemical doping of CNDs to alter the intrinsic electronic properties of materials and provide active sites [34]. The presence of N was also effective to improve QY of fluorescent CNDs [10]. The route of the preparation of the material is shown in Scheme 1 , the product emitted bluish violet fluorescence under the excitation of 365 nm.

To find an optimal condition for the reaction, it is reasonable to use fluorescence spectrometry. The effects of molar ratio of the raw materials, reaction temperature, and reaction time on fluorescence intensity were investigated. When the reaction temperature and reaction time were fixed at 180 °C and 3 h, evident dependence of the fluorescence on the molar ratio of TGCl to CA was observed (Fig. 1 a and Fig. S1). When the molar ratio of TGCl to CA changed from 1:1 and 1:2, the fluorescence intensity increased in the range of 300–350 nm and then decreased in the range of 350–380 nm (Fig. S1a-b). When the molar ratio was lower than 1:3, CNDs exhibited a slightly excitation-dependent characteristic (Fig. S1a-c). When the molar ratio was larger than 1:3, the emission intensity increased as the excitation increased from 300 to 340 nm and decreased with the further increase of the wavelength. The excitation wavelength-dependence became more obvious in the range of 340–380 nm (Fig. S1d-j). The fluorescence spectra of CNDs synthesized under different temperatures were recorded (Fig. S2a-d). The optimal excitation wavelengths were 340 nm for all products obtained under different temperature. 180 °C gave the highest emission intensity (Fig. 1b). Reaction time showed evident influence on the fluorescence property, too (Fig. S3a-e). 3 h gave the highest emission (Fig. 1c).

When excessive TGCl was used, fluorescent products were also obtained, but the emission was not excitation dependent (Fig. S4a-e). Its best excitation was 350 nm and the peak of the emission was at 440 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of the synthesis condition reflected by CE
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the intricate and diverse characteristics in structure and composition of CNDs, the exact origin of photoluminescence phenomenon may need further investigation [9,10,35]. CE analysis of the products indicates that the product contains multiple components. Because CNDs may be nanoparticles with carbonized cores, and may be adsorbed onto the stationary phase of liquid chromatography, capillary zone electrophoresis (CZE) should be an ideal analytical technique to elucidate their composition [20]. Electropherograms of the products synthesized with various molar ratio of TGCl to CA (1:1 to 1:10) are shown in Fig. 2 . Apparently, the molar ratio of the starting materials affected both on the composition and their yields. When the molar ratio of TGCl to CA was 1:1, only a weak peak appeared at a migration time of 435 s. When the molar ratio of TGCl to CA changed to 1:2, a new peak with a migration time of 335 s appeared and the peak after 400 s became bigger. Further change of the molar ratio of TGCl to CA gave more peaks, two of them were higher and their migration times were barely affected by the change of the ratio (marked with yellow and green color strips). There were 2–4 small peaks appeared with significant variation of migration times. Two main peaks at 180 s and 260 s were employed to evaluate the influence of the ratio of the starting materials on fluorescence intensity. The best ratio of TGCl to CA obtained with both peaks was 1:6 (Fig. S5a). CE with UV detector gave more peaks, which demonstrated that some non-fluorescent products formed (Fig. S6a).

Effect of temperature on the electropherograms of the products is shown in Fig. 3 a. Obviously, the reaction temperatures affected both on the composition and their yields. When the temperature was 120 °C, only small amount of fluorescent components were produced, the main peak was at 355 s (marked with purple color strip). At 150 °C, a new peak at 305 s appeared and the other 2 peaks became bigger. When the temperature increased to 180 °C, 2 main peaks at 205 s and 305 s reached their highest and the peak at 355 s decreased dramatically. Further increase of the temperature caused significant decrease of all fluorescent components. Based on the fluorescence intensity of the peak at 205 s and 305 s (marked with yellow and green color strips), the best reaction temperature obtained with the two peaks was 180 °C (Fig. S5b), same as that obtained with the fluorescence spectrometry. Electropherograms of the products synthesized under different reaction times are shown in Fig. 3b. A reaction time of 3 h gave the strongest fluorescence for peaks at 215 s and 320 s (marked with yellow and green color strips) (Fig. S5c). Change of the composition of the products along with the reaction time was observed, main component appeared at a migration time around 380 s (marked with purple color strip) when the reaction time was 2 h or less. Longer reaction time than 5 h caused reduction of the fluorescence of both of the main peaks.

When stoichiometric or excessive TGCl was used, only one fluorescent component was formed (Fig. 3c). The molar ratio of the starting material only affected the yield of the component and the best ratio was 1:2 for CA:TGCl. It should be mentioned that there were non-fluorescent components produced, because CE with UV detection gave 5 peaks (Fig. S6b), which confirmed the complexity of CNDs.

To confirm the consistence of the fluorescence property of these components, electropherograms with different excitation and emission wavelengths were recorded (Fig. 4 ). All main components showed similar excitation and emission behavior, which indicated the similar origin and mechanism of the fluorescence of these components. Because the migration velocity depends on the charge and size, the peaks may be a representation of the difference in size, larger the size, longer the migration time.

The UV–Vis absorption spectra of CNDs prepared with TGCl:CA of 1:6 displayed a characteristic peak centered at 275 nm which may be ascribed to the π-π* transition of CC bond and a shoulder peak at 326 nm (Fig. 5 a) that may be caused by formation of excited defect surface state [20]. Slight excitation wavelength-dependence of the emission was observed (Fig. 5b) and the optimal excitation and emission wavelengths were 340 nm and 410 nm, respectively (Fig. 5a). The relative QY was 17.1% with a reference to quinine sulfate (Fig. S7a). Under irradiation of a 365 nm UV lamp, CNDs in aqueous solution exhibited a bright bluish violet emission. The solution showed completely colorless under the daylight (Inset in Fig. 5a).

The morphology and diameter distribution of the CNDs were characterized by TEM and DLS. The TEM image (Fig. 5c) indicated the presence of near spherical particles with average diameter of 1.1 nm. The average diameter determined by DLS was 2.4 nm (Inset in Fig. 5c). The element analysis demonstrated that C:H ratio changed from 1:1.9 of raw materials to 1:1.3 of the product, indicating a moderate carbonization. The FTIR spectrum (Fig. 5d) showed the absorption at 3431.7 cm−1, which was corresponding to the N–H stretching vibration and O–H stretching vibration [33]. The peak at 1719.9 cm−1 was ascribed to the CO stretching vibration and the peak at 1401.6 cm−1 resulted from C–N stretching vibration. Absorption at 1586.8 cm−1 was due to the asymmetric stretching vibration of carboxylic anions or N–H bending vibration [15]. Four peaks at 285.2, 400.1, 531.3, and 200.0 eV in XPS spectrum were attributed to C 1s, N 1s, O 1s, and Cl element (Fig. 5e), respectively. The peak at 497.1 eV is the Na Auger peak. The high resolution XPS spectrum of C 1s could be resolved into three peaks with binding energy at 282.3, 283.7 and 286.1 eV (Fig. 5f), which were corresponding to C–C/CC, C–N/C–O, and CO/CN, respectively [33,36]. The N 1s spectrum was deconvoluted into three peaks located at 397.2, 398.9, and 400.0 eV (Fig. 5g), which were assigned to N–H, CN, and C3–N [33]. The O 1s spectrum was fitted with two peaks at 529.0 and 530.3 eV (Fig. 5h), which were attributed to CO and C–O [34,37]. These results indicated that CNDs were rich in hydrophilic carboxyl and amino groups.

The fluorescence intensity of the CNDs prepared with TGCl:CA of 1:6 was relatively stable in a pH range of 4.0–10.0 (Fig. 6 a). Decrease in the fluorescence may be caused by protonation of –COOH groups [38] or –NH2 group [39] when pH < 3 and de-protonation of –OH functional group [40] at pH > 11. Same as most of fluorescent materials, the fluorescence decreased with the increase of temperature (Fig. 6b). And the fluorescence intensity shown a linear relationship with the temperature (red line in Fig. 6b). After 300 min of continuous irradiation (wavelength: 340 nm) inside the fluorescence spectrometer, no observable photobleaching was noticed (Fig. 6c).

Cr(VI) caused significant quenching of the fluorescence of the prepared CNDs. The quenching efficiency was affected by pH, and better quenching was obtained in basic medium (Fig. S7b). Considering the simplicity of the operation, pH 8 was used to evaluate the possible interference. 15 kinds of anions (800 μM of each) were examined and none of them had any interference (Fig. 7 a). 16 kinds of cations were evaluated, 100 μM Fe3+ shown slight interference with 12% fluorescence quenching (Fig. 7b).

The mechanism of the response was investigated with CE analysis first. No change of the fluorescent components and their distribution of composition was observed on CE electropherograms. The almost unchanged fluorescence lifetime shown in Fig. S8a before and after addition of Cr(VI) together with CE results excluded the possibility of FRET process. Based on the UV–Vis and fluorescence spectra shown in Fig. S8b-d, inner filter effect (IFE) should be responsible for the fluorescence quenching observed here because of the spectral overlap between the absorption band of the absorber and the excitation spectra and emission spectra of fluorescent material [41]. These results indicated that IFE instead of FRET participated in the quenching procedure [42].

To further explain the IFE mechanism, the percentage of IFE mechanism was calculated based on the principles and equations [41,43]. All equations and related parameters (Scheme S1 and Table S1) were listed in Supporting information. As displayed in Fig. 8 a, the percentage of IFE mechanism for Cr(VI) to CNDs can reach as high as 87%, indicating the IFE mechanism occupied the most part of the fluorescence quenching.

Both of the static quenching and dynamic quenching are fitted for the Stern-Volmer equation [44]:
F 0
F
= 1 +
K
S V
[ Q ]
=
k q
τ 0
[ Q ]
where [Q] is the concentration of Cr(VI), KSV is the Stern-Volmer constant, kq is the quenching rate constant, τ0 is the average fluorescence lifetime of CNDs (τ0 = 9.69 ns). F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensity of CNDs without and with Cr(VI). As shown in Fig. 8b, the Stern-Volmer plots at four temperatures all are linear. The higher temperature, the smaller KSV. So, the quenching is static. Table S2 listed the slope (KSV) and kq (a reflection of quenching efficiency [44]) at different temperatures.
So, IFE and static quenching mechanism are responsible for fluorescence quenching, while the IFE mechanism is predominant.

In summary, novel CNDs were synthesized with the aid of CE analysis for the optimum reaction condition and elucidation of their fluorescent components. Because CE is suitable for analytes from small molecules to large particles, it is very suitable for the analysis of CNDs. CE with fluorescence detector can reflect the fluorescent property of each component, it should be useful if certain components with special property are needed. The results indicated that with excessive CA, components with smaller sizes were produced. The distribution of the size of fluorescent components was not continuous, 4 main peaks appeared on the electropherograms when CA was excessive. In addition, because the migration time is dependent on both the charge and size of the particles, it is expected that CE may be used for determining the size and mechanism of the fluorescence of these components. CNDs as a probe for Cr(VI) detection have the advantages of high sensitivity and easy preparation, while the selectivity still need further improvement. Their application can be extended to other areas that need stable fluorescent materials.

Lizhi Zhao: Conceptualization; Data curation; Investigation; Resources; Methodology; Visualization; Writing – original draft; Validation, Lei Zhao: Software; Validation, Hongli Li: Validation. Jie Ma: Validation. Lei Bian: Validation. Xiayan Wang: Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing, Qiaosheng Pu: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China [Nos. 21575054, 21625501, and 21527808], and the Beijing Outstanding Young Scientists Program [No. BJJWZYJH01201910005017].

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2021.122224.
The authors declare no competing financial interest.
